A palm-sized spring bubbles through white sand, forming a clear pool ringed by watercress and fingerprint-sized patches of emerald moss.
Three flat stones create tiny cascades in a wrist-deep creek, each drop beaded with mist, surrounded by cushions of star moss.
A hollow beneath exposed oak roots forms a small chamber, its earthen floor soft with shed leaves and sprouting wood sorrel.
Maidenhair ferns drape a limestone overhang above a pool no wider than outstretched arms, their stems black against pale stone.
A fallen birch log cradles a garden of oyster mushrooms, their pearl-gray shelves emerging in perfect tiers from papery bark.
Tide pool trapped between black rocks holds three purple anemones, their tentacles swaying in water clear as polished glass.
Sunlight finds a gap in the canopy, illuminating a circle of sword ferns around a rotting stump covered in orange jelly fungus.
A seep darkens the cliff face, painting vertical stripes of rust and green where moisture feeds hanging gardens of liverwort.
Five chanterelles cluster beneath a hemlock's lowest branches, their apricot color vivid against dark duff and scattered needles.
A root hollow opens beneath a cedar, its interior walls lined with licorice fern and floored with dry, rust-colored needles.
Barnacles encrust a channel between tide-pool stones, their volcano shapes still wet, surrounded by pink coralline algae patches.
A meadow the size of a bedroom holds blue lupines and Indian paintbrush, ringed by firs whose lowest branches touch grass.
Water trickles over a stair-step of three moss-covered stones, each emerald cushion thick as a folded blanket, dripping steadily.
A boulder's north face wears a patchwork quilt of lichen: sulfur yellow, seafoam green, and dusty orange overlapping continuously.
Forget-me-nots bloom beside a spring, their sky-blue petals reflected in water that wells up cold through a bed of pebbles.
An alder's exposed roots arch over stream edge, creating a tunnel entrance where water runs dark over smooth, rounded stones.
A nurse log splits open to reveal its red, punky heart, with three hemlock seedlings rising from the decay in perfect spacing.
Behind a boulder, a pencil-thin waterfall drops six feet into a basin lined with black stone and bright green filamentous algae.
Fairy ring mushrooms circle a depression in the forest floor, their tan caps pushing through layers of damp oak leaves.
A rock cleft holds a single maidenhair fern, its delicate fronds backlit by filtered light, roots invisible in shadow.
Willow branches sweep down to touch a still eddy where water striders dimple the surface and reflected leaves shimmer green.
Pink granite holds a shallow pool warmed by sun, its bottom scattered with periwinkle shells and one purple sea star.
A log's bark curls away in long strips, revealing wood beneath carved into galleries by beetles, now home to centipedes.
Sheep sorrel and yarrow grow between stones on a south-facing outcrop, their roots finding purchase in thin accumulations of soil.
A seep wall grows a vertical garden: monkey flowers, saxifrage, and emerald moss fed by water seeping through sandstone layers.
Three turkey tail fungi bracket a fallen alder, their bands of brown and tan perfectly preserved, surfaces velvety to touch.
A hollow stump opens to sky, its interior walls thick with moss, floor holding a pool of rainwater and floating cedar scales.
Cobble beach curves into a small bay where maidenhair kelp washes ashore, its dark ribbons draped over white and gray stones.
A boulder's overhang shelters a patch of walking fern, its fronds touching damp earth, tips rooting to start new plants.
Columbines nod from a crack in cliff face, their red and yellow blooms improbable against gray stone and dusty lichen.
A spring emerges between two roots, its water so clear the sand grains beneath seem magnified, each bubble rising perfectly round.
Bracket fungi climb a dead ash in overlapping tiers, their upper surfaces zoned in cream and gray, undersides pure white.
A tide pool no larger than a dinner plate holds a universe: barnacles, limpets, hermit crabs, and one small sculpin.
Deer fern circles a depression where winter rains collect, their dark fronds arching over water that reflects only sky.
Orange and chartreuse lichen paint a granite face in abstract patterns, each patch crisp-edged where different species meet and compete.
A creek splits around a moss-covered stone, rejoining in a pool where water boatmen skate and caddisfly larvae cling to pebbles.
Beneath a spruce, a circle of coral mushrooms rises like tiny white fingers from duff, each branch tipped with pale yellow.
A sandstone ledge holds a line of shallow pools, each one stepping down to the next, connected by thin, whispering trickles.
Wild ginger leaves carpet the space beneath a log, their heart shapes dark green and glossy, hiding burgundy flowers near roots.
A boulder's south side grows a garden of pale green lichen, their crusty bodies expanding outward in patient, circular growth.
Miner's lettuce fills a shaded alcove, their round leaves like scattered coins on red earth, tiny white flowers on thin stems.
A fallen cedar spans a ravine, its top surface supporting a ridge of moss that runs its entire length like soft green fur.
Stones in a stream bed wear crowns of moss on their upstream faces, each cushion sculpted by current into teardrop shapes.
A root cave's entrance frames a view of creek beyond, its earthen walls hung with roots like brown lace, floor scattered with acorns.
Sea palms cluster in a tide channel, their rubbery stalks bent by waves, holdfasts cemented to rock amid acorn barnacle colonies.
A seep stains the rock wall rusty orange, feeding a hanging mat of moss that drips steadily into a ferny depression below.
Reindeer lichen covers a rotting stump in pale gray-green, their branching forms delicate as coral, crisp and dry to touch.
A natural bridge of roots spans a narrow gully, underneath flows a thread of water over stones stained red with iron.
Indian pipe rises ghostly white from humus beneath hemlocks, their translucent stems and nodding flowers clustered in groups of three.
A pocket meadow holds shooting stars in bloom, their swept-back petals magenta against grass, stamens pointing like tiny yellow beaks.
A thumb-sized cascade spills over velvet moss into a teacup-sized basin, surrounded by delicate sprigs of pearlwort blooming white on wet stone.
Redwood sorrel carpets the space between three surface roots, their shamrock leaves folding closed in shadow, pink-veined and rain-beaded.
A crack in basalt weeps steady moisture, nurturing a single hart's tongue fern whose glossy fronds unfurl from rust-stained rock.
Shelf mushrooms circle a rotting maple stump in perfect gradation, from tender white at top to weathered gray near the moss-thick base.
A miniature gravel bar parts the creek into braids, each channel barely finger-width, water singing different notes over different-sized stones.
Purple sea urchins nestle in a wave-carved hollow, their spines moving slowly, sharing space with turban snails and chiton trails.
Bloodroot leaves push through leaf litter beside a seep, their lobed edges cradling drops of water like silver held in cupped palms.
A boulder's underside creates a cave where wood ferns arch inward, their fronds forming a ceiling over bare earth and scattered snail shells.
Chickweed sprawls across damp ground near a spring outlet, its tiny white star-flowers open to morning, leaves bright lime against dark mud.
A cedar's trunk hollow opens at ground level, interior glowing amber with rot, floor scattered with feathers and lined with fresh moss.
Seven limpets cluster on a single stone in a tide pool, their conical shells worn smooth, surrounded by pink encrusting algae.
A seep channel cuts vertically through sandstone, its groove lined with black moss, water following the path in a whispering thread.
Russula mushrooms push through duff in shades of red and purple, their caps rain-glossed, gills white beneath, scattered around a hemlock trunk.
A fallen branch bridges a handspan creek, its bark colonized by turquoise lichen, moss growing where water mist reaches in morning light.
Kinnikinnick spreads across a rock shelf, its evergreen leaves leathery and small, bearing pink bell flowers above stone painted with map lichen.
A pool between roots holds last night's rain, its surface scattered with alder catkins, bottom lined with decomposing leaves showing delicate veins.
Salal grows dense beneath a clearing edge, its oval leaves layered like shingles, white urn-shaped flowers hanging where sunlight penetrates deepest.
A granite slab tilts to create a narrow slot where bracken fern finds foothold, their triangular fronds forming a screen against weather.
Orange peel fungus fruits from disturbed soil beside a rotted stump, their cups brilliant against black earth, inner surfaces smooth as wax.
A tide pool holds a small world: one ochre star clinging to rock, purple shore crabs beneath algae, water clear to every detail.
Foamflower blooms beside a spring channel, their white bottle-brush flowers nodding over heart-shaped leaves, stamens extending like tiny antennae reaching outward.
A limestone face holds solution pockets filled with soil where succulents root, their fleshy leaves dusty blue-green, flowers yellow against pale stone.
Banana slugs cross a log thick with moss, leaving silver trails that catch light, the wood beneath soft enough to dent with gentle pressure.
A tree root arches over trickling water, forming a bridge where underneath grows a patch of liverwort, its scales flat and geometric.
Puffballs cluster where a log meets earth, their white spheres perfect and unmarked, surrounding wood dark with moisture and studded with beetle holes.
Inside a stump hollow, sword fern grows from the rotted center, fronds spreading outward through the opening like green fountain water frozen mid-splash.
A rock basin holds rainwater gone amber with tannins, surface scattered with hemlock needles, bottom showing stone grain through golden transparency.
Coral bells grow from a cliff seam, their small round leaves catching light, flower stalks rising with bells of cream and pink.
A boulder split by frost creates a vertical garden: moss on one wall, licorice fern on the other, the narrow space between holding only shadow.
Wood ears cluster on dead elder branch, their jelly-like lobes brown and translucent, surfaces ridged like actual ears pressed against weathered bark.
A gravel wash meets the stream edge, its stones sorted by old floods, now grown through with monkey flowers blooming orange and yellow.
Tide recedes to reveal a bench of rock scored with channels, each groove holding trapped water, purple barnacles, and scattered green anemones.
A nurse log hosts a row of young firs, their roots draped over rotting wood like fingers gripping, spaces between revealing orange punk beneath.
Claytonia blooms in a seep zone, their petals white with pink veins, leaves succulent and paired, growing where water keeps soil perpetually damp.
A sandstone overhang drips steadily onto maidenhair fern below, each drop releasing from the same point, creating a divot in stone over centuries.
Artist's conk grows from a dying beech, its bracket form massive and woody, upper surface brown, white underside bearing scratches revealing cream beneath.
A hollow log lies across slope, its interior accessible from both ends, colonized by moss and sprouting three different fern species.
Spring water surfaces through gravel, creating a boil that keeps sand in constant gentle motion, surrounded by a ring of bright watercress.
A boulder's rain shadow creates dry microclimate where reindeer lichen thrives, their pale branches contrasting with dark stone and surrounding green moss.
Rock tripe grows on a north-facing boulder, their leaf-like bodies dark brown and leathery when dry, edges curled upward showing pale undersides.
A pool between tide-worn rocks reflects sky perfectly still, its edges fringed with rockweed, bottom showing purple and orange through gin-clear water.
False lily of the valley carpets ground beneath alders, their paired leaves arranged along stems, white flower spikes rising above like tiny pale torches.
A log's heartwood has rotted hollow, creating a horizontal tunnel lined with pale fungal threads, entrance framed by shelf fungi and curtains of moss.
Stonecrop colonizes a rock outcrop's thin soil, their succulent leaves arranged in rosettes, yellow star flowers open to sun, roots in mineral cracks.
A creek undercuts its bank, creating an overhang where roots dangle free, water dark and slow beneath, carrying leaves into shadow.
Witch's butter grows on dead oak, its gelatinous orange lobes brain-like and trembling, other brackets nearby dried to hard amber by weather.
A pocket between rocks fills with soil where violets bloom, their purple and yellow faces low to ground, leaves heart-shaped and slightly fuzzy.
Silver-dollar lichen patterns a maple branch, their round forms overlapping like coins scattered and glued, edges crisp where they meet bark.
A spring pool's overflow creates a miniature delta on sand, its braided channels constantly shifting, carrying silt grains to form delicate levees.
Earth stars open on disturbed ground, their pointed rays curled back to reveal the spore sac, arrangement geometric and perfect against bare soil.
A limestone pocket cradles a thimble-sized pool where water beetles circle endlessly, bottom lined with calcite crystals and one perfect snail shell.
Polypody ferns sprout from a horizontal crack in shale, their roots invisible in stone, fronds forming a green banner against gray layered rock.
A streamside stone wears a cap of moss shaped by current, downstream side trailing filaments like green hair flowing in clear water.
Underneath a leaning hemlock, ghost pipe clusters push through needle duff, their waxy white stems translucent, nodding heads catching no light.
A hand-sized depression in granite holds yesterday's rain, surface tension perfect, rimmed with granules of orange and silver lichen dust.
Three kinds of moss meet on one boulder face: emerald cushion, golden hair-cap, and sheet moss thin as paper, boundaries sharp.
Tidewater leaves a line of Neptune's necklace seaweed across a rock, each bead-like segment still holding seawater, glossy brown and plump.
A rotting alder hosts chicken of the woods, its sulfur-yellow shelves emerging in fan-shapes, edges still soft and growing outward daily.
Bunchberry blooms in a clearing smaller than a table, their white bracts surrounding tiny flowers, red berries forming where petals have dropped.
A spring surfaces at a tree root's base, water welling up through leaf litter, creating a dark spot that never dries, never spreads.
Cobwebs span between fern fronds in a grotto, each silk strand beaded with fog droplets, turning invisible architecture into pearl necklaces.
A split rock reveals quartz veins running through granite, the opening narrow enough to touch both walls, floor scattered with stone chips.
Scarlet cup fungi bloom from buried wood, their crimson interiors brilliant against brown leaves, outer surfaces pale and slightly fuzzy.
A tide pool's walls support a vertical community: high barnacles, middle mussels, low anemones, each band marking a different water level.
Wood sorrel grows beneath a root overhang, their clover leaves casting shamrock shadows on bare earth when afternoon sun angles inward.
A seep creates a wet streak down rock, supporting a descending garden: moss at top, liverwort middle, algae at bottom where water pools.
Miniature cascade drops through three stair-steps of stone, each level holding a pool barely deep enough for a fingertip, water perpetually tumbling.
A boulder field's smallest chamber holds dry leaves and one perfect bird nest, roof formed by leaning stones, entrance facing away from weather.
Purple rock cress blooms from a cliff crack, their four-petaled flowers improbable against vertical stone, roots finding moisture in hairline fractures.
A log's bark has peeled in rectangular plates, revealing wood carved into channels by beetle larvae, patterns geometric and surprisingly deliberate.
Tide retreat reveals a boulder garden: acorn barnacles, limpet tracks through algae film, one small octopus visible in a shadowed crevice beneath.
A cedar root arches over nothing, the soil beneath long since washed away, creating a window frame through which stream water glints.
Pinesap plants cluster where a pine once stood, their red stems leafless and waxy, flowers hanging like bells, drawing life from fungal threads.
A rock face weeps through multiple points, each seep supporting its own moss species, creating a vertical patchwork of green textures and tones.
Inside a hollow log's curve, a wren has woven a nest from moss and rootlets, the structure fitting perfectly against rotted wood's contour.
A boulder split reveals its interior: feldspar crystals catching light, mica sheets reflecting silver, quartz veins running through like frozen lightning.
Tide pool anemones withdraw at low water, their tentacles hidden, bodies looking like burgundy lumps until submersion brings them back to bloom.
A spring channel cuts through clay, its banks vertical and smooth, bottom paved with leaves arranged by current into overlapping shingles downstream.
Dead man's fingers fungus rises from buried wood, their club-shaped bodies black and slightly rough, tips rounded, growing in groups of five.
A natural bridge of intertwined roots spans a gully, underneath showing layers of leaf litter and the glint of seasonal water on stone.
Sea lettuce drapes across tide pool rocks, their translucent green sheets thin as tissue, edges ruffled, torn by waves into irregular shapes.
A rotting stump's top has become a garden: moss, seedlings, and one spider web strung between sprouting hemlocks like a catch-net for seeds.
Alumroot grows from a rock seam, their rounded leaves on long stems forming a low canopy, flower stalks rising with tiny bells of white.
A log bridge across a creek supports its own ecosystem: moss above, hanging roots below, salamanders visible in dark water underneath.
Stone eroded by water reveals honeycomb texture, each pocket holding a miniature world: moss, seeds, one millipede coiled in perfect spiral sleep.
Chanterelle mushrooms emerge through duff beneath oak, their funnel shapes golden, ridges running down stems, scattered in a loose constellation pattern.
A boulder's lee side stays perpetually damp, supporting a hanging garden where baby tears sprawl across stone like green embroidery on gray cloth.
Tide channel holds water even at low tide, its walls close enough to touch simultaneously, floor showing purple urchins and orange sea stars.
Inside a root hollow, last year's leaves have compressed into layers showing their decay progression: brown to gray to black to soil.
Toadstools ring a depression filled with rain, their red caps white-spotted and perfect, emerging from grass in a circle suggesting buried wood below.
A seep zone supports sundew plants, their round leaves sticky with dew drops, each hair tipped with adhesive waiting for insects that never come.
Rock face painted by iron and manganese: rust-red streaks alternate with black, minerals deposited by water following invisible paths through stone decades long.
A log's interior has rotted to punk while bark remains intact, creating a shell that crumbles to show orange interior when pressed gently.
Tide pool holds four species of algae: pink coralline crust, green sea lettuce ribbons, brown rockweed, red dulse, each claiming separate territory.
Spring water surfaces at cliff base, immediately disappearing into gravel, its brief appearance marked by watercress and one perfect fern growing sideways.
A nurse log supports hemlock seedlings in a line, their roots reaching down through moss and rot toward mineral soil still feet below.
Saxifrage blooms from a seep's edge, their white flowers on red stems nodding where water keeps rock perpetually wet and stained with minerals.
Inside a stump hollow, rain has created a pool that never empties, its surface scattered with hemlock scales, bottom showing advanced wood decay.
A boulder tumble creates dozens of small caves, each with different conditions: dry with lichen, damp with moss, wet with ferns, dark with beetles.
Earthstars dried and open on sandy soil, their pointed rays spread flat like compasses, central sacs releasing spore puffs when raindrops strike them.
A finger-width stream braids around pebbles wearing lichen like patina, each stone creating miniature rapids that whisper different pitches in continuous harmony.
Underneath a maple's buttress roots, soil has eroded to reveal a chamber floored with amber leaves, ceiling woven from hairlike rootlets.
A limestone alcove weeps moisture through porous rock, feeding cushions of golden moss that glow when backlit by afternoon sun filtering through.
Crimson waxcap mushrooms push through needle duff in a tight cluster, their glossy caps reflecting light like wet paint against dark substrate.
Between two tide-worn boulders, a slot canyon in miniature holds trapped seawater, walls lined with chitons and periwinkles in vertical columns.
A seep emerges between shale layers, creating a wet stripe where miniature ferns colonize the vertical surface in descending size order.
Hollow log interior reveals wood grain carved into ridges by rot, galleries filled with carpenter ant debris and shed beetle carapaces perfectly preserved.
Spring bubbles up through clay, forming a dome of water that breaks surface tension before flowing away through a channel it carved itself.
A granite outcrop's depression catches rain, holding a temporary pool where caddisfly larvae have built cases from tiny quartz grains and mica flakes.
Underneath exposed spruce roots, earth has washed away leaving a curtain of danglers, their tips still searching for soil long gone.
A boulder's overhang shelters a patch of moss so thick it holds bootprints, surface stippled with capsules releasing spores like green smoke.
Dead alder supports a cascade of turkey tails, each shelf perfectly semicircular, banded in colors graduating from cream to chocolate to charcoal.
Tide pool no bigger than a handprint contains a perfect ecosystem: three hermit crabs, purple shore crab, six limpets, endless barnacles.
A creek side-channel dead-ends in a pool surrounded by skunk cabbage, their yellow spathes emerging through mud like hooded monks in council.
Boulder split by ice reveals crystal-lined interior, the gap narrow enough to create twilight within, floor scattered with stone fragments and one bird bone.
Underneath a log jam, water has carved a cave in the bank where roots hang free, their tips dripping in permanent darkness.
A seep stains sandstone in vertical bands of rust and black, minerals painting stripes where different chemistries emerge from separate layers.
Miniature clearing holds wild strawberries in fruit, their red berries hidden under trifoliate leaves, surrounded by a stockade of salal taller than.
Between rocks, a soil pocket supports one perfect trillium, its three-petaled white flower nodding above three leaves arranged in symmetrical precision.
A root hollow opens to creek view, its chamber sized for one person curled small, walls decorated with shelf fungus and draperies of moss.
Tide channel connects two pools, its floor paved with mussels so densely packed their blue-black shells form cobblestones, water rushing overhead at high tide.
Spring seep creates a wet zone where horsetails grow in ranks, their segmented stems perfectly vertical, spore cones forming at tips like tiny pineapples.
A log's bark hosts three lichen species competing for space, their boundaries sharp where different chemical defenses prevent overlap and mixing.
Boulder garden in miniature: hens and chicks colonizing a stone face, their geometric rosettes expanding outward in patient circles toward neighboring plants.
Underneath a leaning cedar, space opens where needles never fall, bare earth holds only one thing, a cluster of rattlesnake plantain in permanent shade.
Creek undercuts a stone ledge, creating an overhang where swallows have nested, mud cups empty now, sticks and feathers scattered on gravel below.
A rotted log reveals chambers carved by termites, their galleries architectural and complex, walls paper-thin between rooms connected by precise tunnels.
Seep zone supports a descending ecosystem: moss at source, then ferns, then horsetails, then sedges where water finally spreads into boggy ground.
Between boulders, a slot fills with leaves each autumn, compressing over years into layers readable like tree rings in horizontal stripes.
Tide exposes a rock bench scored with parallel grooves, each channel holding different seaweeds arranged by depth like crops in rows.
A nurse log splits lengthwise, revealing its red heart still solid while sapwood crumbles, three seedlings growing from the crack between halves.
Spring emerges at tree base, its water so cold it creates visible current in the warm air, flowing over roots polished smooth by decades.
Underneath a boulder overhang, perpetual shade supports a garden of prince's pine, their evergreen whorls surrounding stems topped with nodding pink flowers.
Creek deposits sand in an eddy, forming a crescent beach smaller than a bath mat, surface marked with bird tracks and one perfect snail shell.
A log's end shows growth rings, each annual band visible, outer rings supporting moss while heartwood remains hard, center hollow where rot began.
Seep creates a hanging garden on cliff face, maidenhair fern and baby tears cascading down like green waterfall frozen in place by roots.
Boulder field creates a maze at ground level, passages between stones just wide enough to navigate, floors carpeted with shed maple seeds.
Tide pool holds a small octopus visible in a crevice, its skin color-shifting from red to brown, one eye watching through crystal water.
A root cave opens under an ancient oak, its entrance framed by bracket fungus, interior holding only darkness and the sound of dripping.
Spring channel cuts through forest floor, its banks lined with monkey flowers blooming orange, water running over fallen leaves arranged like roof tiles.
Between two stones, soil pocket supports a clump of coral root orchids, their stems leafless and waxy red, flowers striped burgundy and cream.
Underneath a log, salamanders shelter in the damp space, their orange spots vivid against black skin, bodies motionless except for breathing flanks.
Creek creates a side pool where water barely moves, surface covered in duckweed forming a green carpet, one frog nose breaking through like periscope.
A boulder's north face grows a vertical garden: moss low, then lichen middle, then bare rock high where sun and wind prevent colonization.
Seep emerges from cliff crack, water running down to pool at base where watercress grows in spreading mats, roots white in clear shallow.
Log interior has become soil, supporting a row of tiny hemlocks, their roots reaching down through rot toward mineral earth still inches below.
Tide retreat reveals rock platform with a single large anemone, its tentacles extended like flower petals, green and purple in shallow remaining water.
A stump hollow fills with rainwater that reflects sky, bottom lined with decomposed wood fine as coffee grounds, one newt swimming slow circles.
Between granite slabs, a crack widens to hand width, its interior walls crusted with crystals catching light, floor holding only shadow and one spider.
Creek bank erodes to show soil layers: black humus on top, then brown loam, then gray clay, then rounded stones, each stratum telling formation story.
